seo.title: Trat: Complete Travel Guide 2026 — Top Things To Do
seo.metaDescription: Plan your Trat trip with our complete 2026 guide. Koh Chang, Koh Kood, Koh Mak, waterfalls, night markets, and island hopping. Transport from Bangkok, best hotels, and practical tips.

overview: Trat is Thailand's easternmost province and the gateway to the Koh Chang archipelago — 52 islands scattered across the Gulf of Thailand near the Cambodian border. While most visitors rush through Trat Town to catch a ferry, the province rewards those who slow down. The charming old town hides century-old wooden shophouses, a bustling nightly food market, and one of Thailand's last gem-trading centers. On the islands, Koh Chang delivers beaches and jungle waterfalls as Thailand's second-largest island. Koh Kood (Kut) is the unspoiled beauty — pristine beaches with a fraction of Koh Chang's visitors. Koh Mak offers a car-free cycling paradise between the two. Back on the mainland, Trat province is Thailand's durian capital, and the lush mangrove forests along the coast shelter rich ecosystems. Whether you're island-hopping by speedboat, swimming under jungle waterfalls, or eating freshly caught seafood at a night market, Trat offers an authentic eastern Thailand experience far from the Andaman coast crowds.
highlights: Koh Chang — Thailand's second-largest island with white-sand beaches, jungle waterfalls, and a national marine park
Koh Kood (Kut) — one of Thailand's most beautiful and least developed islands with crystal-clear water and empty beaches
Koh Mak — a small, car-free island perfect for cycling, kayaking, and total relaxation
Klong Plu Waterfall — Koh Chang's most popular waterfall with a natural swimming pool at the base, inside the national park
Trat Night Market — a nightly street food market in the old town with local seafood, curries, and tropical fruits at Thai prices
Mu Ko Chang National Park — a marine national park covering 52 islands with snorkeling, diving, and coral reefs
Trat Old Town — atmospheric wooden shophouses, narrow lanes along the river, and century-old architecture
Island hopping by ferry — Boonsiri catamaran connects Koh Chang, Koh Mak, and Koh Kood in a single day trip
Durian and mangosteen orchards — Trat is Thailand's durian capital with seasonal fruit festivals (Apr-Jun)
Mangrove forest boardwalks — explore coastal mangrove ecosystems along Trat's mainland coast

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov-Feb: Dry season with clear skies, calm seas, and ideal conditions for island hopping, diving, and beach time. December-January is busiest with highest hotel prices on Koh Chang. All ferry routes operate at full frequency.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar-May: Still dry but increasingly hot (35C+). Fewer tourists, hotel prices drop 20-30%. Durian season peaks April-June — the best time for fruit lovers. Seas remain calm for island tours.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun-Oct: Southwest monsoon brings afternoon rain showers and rougher seas. Some ferry routes to Koh Mak and Koh Kood are reduced or suspended. Koh Chang remains accessible year-round. Hotel prices drop 40-60%. Many resorts on Koh Kood close entirely.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for guaranteed beach weather and full island access. March-May is excellent for budget travelers and fruit lovers. Avoid Koh Kood and Koh Mak in June-September when ferry service is unreliable — Koh Chang and Trat Town are fine year-round.

topAttractions.name: Koh Chang Beaches
topAttractions.description: Thailand's second-largest island offers a string of west-coast beaches, each with its own character. White Sand Beach (Hat Sai Khao) is the most developed with resorts and nightlife. Klong Prao is the longest and most peaceful. Kai Bae has a relaxed village feel. Lonely Beach (Hat Tha Nam) is the backpacker hub with cheap bungalows and beach bars. The east coast is quieter and less developed, with fishing villages and mangrove forests.
topAttractions.location: Koh Chang, 30 min ferry from mainland
tips: White Sand Beach is best for first-timers — most facilities and easiest to navigate
Lonely Beach has the cheapest accommodation and best backpacker vibe
East coast is less touristy but you'll need a scooter to explore

topAttractions.name: Klong Plu Waterfall
topAttractions.description: Koh Chang's most popular and accessible waterfall, set inside Mu Ko Chang National Park. A 500-meter trail through jungle leads to a three-tiered waterfall with a deep natural swimming pool at the base. The pool is large enough for swimming and the cool mountain water is refreshing after the tropical heat. Located near Klong Prao Beach on the west coast.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB (~$6 USD) for foreigners, 100 THB children (national park fee)
tips: Visit early morning to avoid crowds — gets busy by 11 AM
The same national park ticket works for Than Mayom Waterfall on the same day
Wear water shoes — rocks around the pool are slippery

topAttractions.name: Koh Kood (Ko Kut)
topAttractions.description: Thailand's fourth-largest island and arguably its most beautiful. Pristine white-sand beaches, crystal-clear water, and zero mass tourism — Koh Kood is what Koh Samui looked like 30 years ago. Klong Chao Beach and Ao Tapao are stunning. A jungle waterfall (Klong Chao Waterfall) feeds a natural pool. The island has no ATMs and limited nightlife — come here to disconnect. Accessible by speedboat from Laem Sok pier (1.5 hours) or via Koh Chang.
topAttractions.location: Koh Kood, 1.5 hrs speedboat from Laem Sok
tips: Bring enough cash — there are no ATMs on Koh Kood
Most resorts close June-October when ferries are reduced
Rent a scooter (300 THB/day) to explore the island — it's the only practical transport

topAttractions.name: Koh Mak
topAttractions.description: A small, flat island between Koh Chang and Koh Kood that's perfect for cycling. No cars, no 7-Elevens, just beaches, coconut palms, and rubber plantations connected by flat roads ideal for bicycles. Ao Suan Yai beach on the west coast has the best sunset views. Snorkeling off the southern coast reveals healthy coral reefs. Koh Mak is the most relaxed island in the archipelago — a genuine escape from everything.
topAttractions.location: Koh Mak, 1 hr speedboat from Laem Ngop
tips: Rent a bicycle (50-100 THB/day) — the island is flat and small enough to cycle in an hour
Book accommodation in advance during peak season — there are limited options
The island is a designated Low Carbon Destination by the Thai government

topAttractions.name: Than Mayom Waterfall
topAttractions.description: A historic waterfall on Koh Chang's east coast, visited by King Rama V and King Rama VII who carved their royal initials into the rocks. A scenic trail through dense jungle leads to three tiers of cascading water. Less crowded than Klong Plu but equally beautiful. The royal carvings are visible near the second tier and add a unique historical dimension.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB (~$6 USD) for foreigners (national park fee, same ticket as Klong Plu)
tips: Look for King Rama V's initials carved into the rock near the second tier
Less touristy than Klong Plu — better for a quieter experience
The trail can be slippery in wet season — wear proper shoes

topAttractions.name: Trat Night Market
topAttractions.description: A nightly street food market in Trat's old town that draws locals and travelers alike. Stalls line Tha Ruea Road selling fresh seafood, curries, grilled meats, tropical fruits, and Thai desserts at genuinely local prices (30-80 THB per dish). The atmosphere is authentically Thai — you'll often be the only foreigner. Open every evening from around 5 PM to 9 PM.
topAttractions.location: Tha Ruea Road, Trat Old Town
tips: Try the grilled seafood skewers and som tam (papaya salad) — among the cheapest in Thailand
The market is busiest on weekends but runs every night
Bring small bills — most vendors don't carry much change

topAttractions.name: Snorkeling & Diving at Koh Rang
topAttractions.description: Koh Rang is the crown jewel of the Mu Ko Chang National Park for underwater exploration. The uninhabited island group has some of the best coral reefs in the Gulf of Thailand, with visibility reaching 20+ meters in high season. Full-day snorkeling tours from Koh Chang visit multiple stops around Koh Rang, Koh Yak, and Koh Laoya. Dive shops on Koh Chang offer PADI courses and fun dives.
topAttractions.location: Koh Rang, 1-2 hrs by boat from Koh Chang
topAttractions.entranceFee: Snorkeling tours 800-1,500 THB (~$22-42 USD), national park fee 200 THB additional
tips: Best visibility November to April — avoid monsoon months for diving
Koh Rang is uninhabited — bring everything you need including water and snacks
PADI Open Water courses on Koh Chang start from around 12,000 THB

topAttractions.name: Trat Old Town
topAttractions.description: The narrow lanes along the Trat River hide century-old wooden shophouses, small temples, and a gem-trading heritage. Trat was historically a center for sapphire and ruby trading, and a few gem shops still operate. The old town's wooden architecture, quiet riverside atmosphere, and lack of tourist infrastructure make it feel like stepping back in time. The Trat Museum and several small temples are worth exploring on foot.
tips: Walk the narrow lanes near the river for the best old wooden houses
The gem market is small but authentic — buy from established shops with certificates
Combine with the night market for a full evening in town

travelTips: Trat Town is just a transit point for most travelers — but the night market and old town are worth an overnight stay before heading to the islands
Ferry schedules to Koh Mak and Koh Kood are drastically reduced June-October — check Boonsiri Ferry schedules before booking island accommodation
Koh Chang has 7-Elevens, ATMs, and hospitals — Koh Kood has none of these. Stock up before heading to the outer islands
Rent a scooter on Koh Chang (250-300 THB/day) — the island is large and songthaew service is infrequent and expensive
The road over Koh Chang's interior mountains is steep and winding — drive carefully, especially on scooters after rain
Bring cash to Koh Kood — there are no ATMs and many places don't accept cards
Durian season (April-June) is the best time for fruit lovers — roadside stalls in Trat province sell premium durian at half the Bangkok price
The ferry from the mainland to Koh Chang runs every 45 minutes and takes 30 minutes — no advance booking needed
Koh Mak is small enough to cycle in an hour — skip the scooter and rent a bicycle instead
Mosquitoes are fierce in the jungle and at waterfalls — bring strong repellent, especially during wet season
gettingThere: From Bangkok, take a bus from Ekkamai (Eastern) Bus Terminal to Trat — departures every 30-60 minutes from 05:00 to 23:00, 5-6 hours, 250-350 THB (~$7-10 USD). Bangkok Airways flies Bangkok (BKK) to Trat (TDX) daily in about 1 hour from 2,500 THB one-way. From Trat, songthaews run to the ferry piers. The Ao Thammachat car ferry to Koh Chang runs every 45 minutes (06:30-18:30, 30-minute crossing, 80 THB per person). For Koh Kood and Koh Mak, speedboats depart from Laem Sok and Laem Ngop piers (1-1.5 hours, 600-1,200 THB).
gettingAround: On Koh Chang, songthaews run along the west coast road but are infrequent (50-100 THB per trip) — renting a scooter (250-300 THB/day) is strongly recommended. Trat Town is compact and walkable. On Koh Mak, bicycles (50-100 THB/day) are the best way around the flat island. On Koh Kood, scooter rental (300 THB/day) is the only practical option. Boonsiri Ferry runs inter-island service: Koh Chang (Bang Bao) → Koh Mak → Koh Kood for 750-1,200 THB. Taxis from Trat airport to ferry piers cost 280 THB per person including ferry ticket.
